html:
Side-by-side pinhole camera
(a) Rays overlap on the wall. (b) A pinhole selects one ray from each object point, forming an inverted image.
⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: large, bold cartoon tree (distinct canopy and trunk) as the scene object
projection wall (light-colored rectangular plane facing the camera)
foreground opaque black barrier with a single small circular pinhole at its center
rays: (a) broad fan of rays from every part of the tree to the wall (no pinhole case); (b) set of rays from tree through pinhole to wall (pinhole case)
projected inverted image of the tree on the projection wall (sharp if pinhole, diffuse if not)
scene label overlays: 'Tree', 'Wall', 'Pinhole Barrier', 'Pinhole'
side-by-side split: (a) wall with no pinhole; (b) wall with pinhole (both simultaneously visible with clear dividing line)
optional arrow annotations showing light paths in both 'no pinhole' and 'pinhole' halves
floor grid for depth cues
direction axis annotation (left–right, depth, up)

interactions.label: Split view
interactions.teaches: Shows both cases side-by-side — wall with no pinhole (no image appears) versus wall with pinhole barrier (image forms), directly comparing the effect of the pinhole.

interactions.label: Screen distance
interactions.teaches: Dragging this changes the distance from the pinhole to the projection wall, which controls the size (and sharpness) of the inverted tree image.

interactions.label: Tree position
interactions.teaches: Moving the tree closer or farther shows how the projected image adjusts in size and brightness: a closer tree creates a bigger projection.

demo_steps.title: Side-by-side: wall vs. pinhole
demo_steps.narration: Compare both scenarios: on the left, the wall receives light from all parts of the tree and no image forms — just uniform illumination. On the right, the pinhole barrier allows only those rays passing through the tiny opening, projecting a bold, inverted image of the tree on the wall.

demo_steps.title: Focus on pinhole image
demo_steps.narration: Hide the split and view only the pinhole setup. See how the pinhole barrier blocks most rays, so each wall point sees light from exactly one spot on the tree, producing a crisp, upside-down image. The cartoon tree's top becomes the bottom of the wall image!

demo_steps.title: Move the screen further: image enlarges
demo_steps.narration: Slide the wall farther from the pinhole. The projected tree image grows bigger and dimmer — the geometric rays spread out over a larger area, making the inverted tree stretch across the wall. This demonstrates how image scale is set by the screen's distance from the pinhole.

demo_steps.title: Tree farther: smaller, fainter image
demo_steps.narration: Now move the cartoon tree farther away from the pinhole, keeping the wall fixed. The projection on the wall shrinks — distant objects appear smaller and fainter in the pinhole image, just as in real life. The upright tree outside produces a smaller upside-down image on the wall inside.

plan.camera_suggestion: Perspective view from above and slightly offset, wide enough to show both tree and wall in 3D, with the pinhole barrier between. For split view, center the dividing line and keep all geometric relationships clear and cartoon-scale bold. Zoom to keep the entire pinhole-wall-tree setup visible, matching the axis proportions and playful framing of the textbook diagram.
plan.notes: In splitView=true, render two setups side-by-side: (a) no pinhole (wall evenly lit, rays from all tree points overlap and blur), (b) pinhole (rays only from pinhole, sharp inverted tree image). The tree graphic should use saturated color and clearly separated trunk/canopy volumes for cartoon clarity. The inverted image must flip both vertically and horizontally. When splitView=false, only render the pinhole setup at center. Use semi-transparent or color-coded rays for didactic clarity. Suggest gentle camera orbit on user hover to reinforce spatial relationships. Use THREE.Mesh with cartoon shading for the tree and walls; draw rays with THREE.Line segments. Render projected tree as a colored, semi-transparent 2D mask/texture mapped onto the screen surface.

plan.contextChunk:
set of measurements of intensities coming from different directions in
space, and thus from different surfaces in the world.

Perhaps the simplest camera is a **pinhole camera**. A pinhole camera
requires a light-tight enclosure, a small hole that lets light pass, and
a projection surface where one senses or views the illumination
intensity as a function of position. @fig-wallpicture (b) shows the the geometry of a scene,
the pinhole, and a projection surface (wall). For any given point on the
projection surface, the light that falls there comes from only from one
direction, along the straight line joining the surface position and the
pinhole. This creates an image of what's in the world on the projection
plane.

![(a) Why there are no pictures appearing on the walls? (b) The pinhole
camera restricts the light rays reaching the wall, producing an image to
appear.](figures/imaging/no_picture_on_a_wall_aina.png){#fig-wallpicture
width="100%"}

Making pinhole cameras is easy and it is a good exercise to gain an
intuition of the image projection process. The picture in @fig-pinhole3 shows a very
simple setup similar to the diagram from @fig-wallpicture (b). This setup is formed by two
pieces of paper, one with a hole on it. With the right illumination
conditions you can see an image projected on the white piece of paper in
front of the opening. You can use this very simple setup to see the
effect of changing the distance between the projection plane and the
pinhole.

![A simple setting for creating images on a white piece of paper. In
front of the white piece of paper we place another piece of black paper
with a hole in the middle. The black paper projects a shadow on the
white paper and, in the middle of the shadow, appears a picture of the

elements: 3D tree object positioned in front of a wall
projection wall (rectangular plane perpendicular to view direction)
set of light rays coming from various points on the tree toward the wall
pinhole (small circular aperture on a black screen)
opaque wall between tree and projection wall, with a single pinhole at center
set of light rays passing through the pinhole and reaching corresponding points on the projection wall
projected (inverted) image of the tree on the projection wall
scene labels: 'Wall', 'Pinhole'
axis annotation to show direction from tree through pinhole to wall

interactions.label: Show pinhole barrier
interactions.teaches: Switches between the case where all rays reach the wall (no image forms) and the case where only rays passing through the pinhole reach the wall (image formation).

interactions.label: Screen distance
interactions.teaches: Changing the distance between the pinhole and the screen controls the size and sharpness of the projected image.

interactions.label: Tree position
interactions.teaches: Moving the tree closer or farther changes how its projection appears—farther trees produce smaller images on the wall, closer trees produce larger ones.

demo_steps.title: No pinhole, no image
demo_steps.narration: Initially, the pinhole barrier is hidden. Light rays from many directions all hit the wall, causing an even illumination with no image. There is nothing to focus the light into a picture of the scene.

demo_steps.title: Enable pinhole, picture forms
demo_steps.narration: Now the pinhole barrier appears. Only light rays that pass through the tiny pinhole reach the wall, and each wall point receives light from just one direction. This restriction causes an inverted image of the tree to appear on the projection wall.

demo_steps.title: Move the screen back: image enlarges
demo_steps.narration: Sliding the screen farther from the pinhole makes the projected image grow larger, but also dimmer and potentially blurrier. The geometry shows how the image size is proportional to the screen distance.

demo_steps.title: Change tree distance: image shrinks
demo_steps.narration: Now move the tree farther away. The projected image of the tree on the wall shrinks, since the rays spread less by the time they reach the screen. This mimics how distant objects appear smaller.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Wide-angle view slightly above and to the side, capturing the tree, the pinhole barrier, and the projection wall in one perspective. Ensure all rays and the inverted image are clearly visible.
interactionPlan.notes: When showPinholeBarrier is false, show several rays from different parts of the tree hitting the wall directly; no image appears, only overlapping rays. When true, show only rays passing through the pinhole and project the inverted image on the wall (optionally, semi-transparent overlays for visualization). The projected image must flip vertically and horizontally relative to the tree. Use Three.js Mesh for the tree and wall, and THREE.Line for rays.

attempts.html:
No pinhole, no image
Many rays overlap on the wall, so no picture is selected.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The screenshot only shows the left (a) panel, not both (a) and (b) side-by-side as in the reference.
No visible pinhole barrier or projection with inverted tree (panel b) is present in the screenshot.
The tree is smaller and less visually prominent compared to the source image.
Some branch and crown shapes and proportions differ from the reference's stylized tree.
The wall perspective and border thickness are slightly different.
evaluation.notes: Solid interactive controls and labeling, but the current view omits the essential pinhole/imaging part and side-by-side panels from the source.
action_items: Default to a split view or animation that clearly shows both the no-pinhole and pinhole (projection) cases side-by-side to match the reference.
Ensure the pinhole barrier, its circular aperture, and the inverted projected tree are visible in at least one step or view.
Increase tree scale and stylize trunk/canopy shapes to better match the bold, cartoonish look of the source.
Adjust camera framing to better echo the original figure's clear geometric relationships and perspective.
Add step/caption guidance to explicitly draw attention to the transition between configuration (a) and (b).

feedback.rationale: The critique identifies conceptual misunderstandings such as omitting the essential side-by-side pinhole/projection cases and missing structural elements, indicating the plan needs fundamental revision.

attempts.html:
Side-by-side pinhole camera
(a) Rays overlap on the wall. (b) A pinhole selects one ray from each object point, forming an inverted image.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The color and composition of the tree and its image are not perfectly matched to the 2D figure (e.g., tree shading/leaf gradients differ).
The wall in the 3D figure includes subtle shadowing and reflected sides not present in the 2D image.
The style and structure of the construction rays are more complex (density and color variation) than the simpler, uniformly shaped arrows in the 2D reference.
Some labels and analytics (tooltips, guidance boxes) are present that are not in the 2D source, potentially shifting focus.
The trunk base and pinhole wall labeling styles diverge from the 2D figure's visual clarity and exact positioning.
evaluation.notes: Exceptional interactivity and conceptual accuracy, with minor geometric and stylistic discrepancies compared to the original 2D illustration.
action_items: Adjust the tree geometry and coloring to match the simpler, flatter style of the 2D reference (reduce gradients/details).
Refine arrows/rays to use solid colors and consistent thickness, more closely matching the original figure's uniform style.
Move, resize, or re-style the labels to more precisely match the source in font, size, and placement.
Consider simplifying wall geometry—remove reflective side panels and excessive shadowing to stay true to the flat 2D look.
Tweak the pinhole barrier and image colors for even greater visual fidelity to the source.

feedback.rationale: All issues are stylistic or implementation-specific (geometry, labels, color fidelity), with no indication of a conceptual or planning error.
action_items: Adjust the tree geometry and coloring to match the simpler, flatter style of the 2D reference (reduce gradients/details).

attempts.html:
Side-by-side pinhole camera
(a) Rays overlap on the wall. (b) A pinhole selects one ray from each object point, forming an inverted image.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The tree in both panels is larger and more cartoonish than in the reference, with exaggerated trunk and roots.
Some leaf colors and overlaps do not match the soft translucency and coloring in the reference.
Rays have a more uniform/algorithmic spread, lacking the hand-drawn variety and casual look of the reference image.
The pinhole in panel (b) is clearly visible, but the realistic light patching/fading on the wall is more schematic than the artistic blur in the reference.
Extra interactive UI overlays are visible in the rendering, partially obscuring the visual clarity of the rays and scene.
evaluation.notes: The figure captures the core dual-panel pinhole camera concept with strong interactivity and mostly accurate geometry, though proportions, coloring, and fine artistic details diverge from the original.
action_items: Refine the tree geometry and trunk shape to more closely match the proportions and simpler form of the source image.
Soften the coloring, translucency, and overlap of the leaves for a more accurate visual texture.
Reduce interface/control clutter or fade out UI during screenshot/export.
Add more natural variation to the ray directions and endpoints for a less mechanical look.
Fine-tune positioning of panels and walls so the composition and spatial relations match the original.

feedback.rationale: All issues concern visual style, geometry refinement, color accuracy, and UI clutter, indicating the plan is correct but the implementation needs execution improvements.
